faq.title: Frequently asked questions
faq.sub: Everything you need to know before you sign up.

q1.q: Is JW Study affiliated with the Watch Tower Society or jw.org?
q1.a: No. JW Study is an independent tool built by a publisher to help fellow Witnesses track Bible reading and study. We are not endorsed by, sponsored by, or connected to the Watch Tower Bible and Tract Society. All Bible text and references link out to the official jw.org and JW Library.

q2.a: Yes. JW Study is 100% free, no trial, no card, no hidden tiers. Everything on the site is free to use.

q3.q: Do I need to create an account to try it?
q3.a: No. You can use the full Bible reading tracker without signing up, just visit Try the tracker, check off chapters, and your progress is saved on your device. Create a free account when you're ready to sync across devices and unlock streaks, notes, and reading plans.

q4.a: Yes. Your reading progress, notes, and study data are private to you by default. We never share or sell your data. You can delete your account and all data at any time.

q5.q: Does it work offline / on my phone?
q5.a: Yes. JW Study is a Progressive Web App, install it on iPhone, Android, or desktop with one tap. It works like a native app, no App Store needed, and stays in sync across all your devices.

q6.q: What if I'm not a Jehovah's Witness?
q6.a: You're welcome here too. JW Study is built around the New World Translation, but anyone studying the Bible is free to use it.

bookSummaries: Genesis records the creation of the universe, the fall of humanity, the flood, and the calling of Abraham, establishing the foundation of God's redemptive plan.
Exodus tells the dramatic story of Israel's deliverance from Egypt through Moses, the giving of the Ten Commandments, and the construction of the Tabernacle.
Leviticus provides detailed instructions for Israel's worship, sacrifices, and priestly duties, centered on the call to be holy because God is holy.
Numbers chronicles Israel's 40-year wilderness journey, their repeated failures, and God's faithful provision and discipline as He guides them toward Canaan.
Deuteronomy is Moses' farewell address to Israel before entering Canaan, recalling their history and renewing the covenant with a call to love and obey God.
Joshua records Israel's conquest and settlement of Canaan under Joshua's leadership, showing God's faithfulness in fulfilling His promises to Abraham.
Judges documents a recurring pattern in Israel: they abandon God, face oppression, cry out, and God raises up a deliverer. It reveals humanity's need for a righteous king.
Ruth is a beautiful story of loyal love set during the time of the judges. Ruth's faithfulness to Naomi becomes a picture of God's redeeming grace.
First Samuel covers the transition from judges to kings: the ministry of Samuel, the failed reign of Saul, and the rise of David as God's chosen king.
Second Samuel recounts David's reign in full (his triumphs, his sin with Bathsheba, and the consequences that follow) while showing God's covenant faithfulness.
First Kings covers Solomon's glorious reign, the building of the Temple, and the tragic division of the kingdom, setting the stage for Israel's long decline.
Second Kings records the progressive decline and fall of both Israel (to Assyria) and Judah (to Babylon), showing the consequences of persistent unfaithfulness.
First Chronicles retells Israel's history from a priestly perspective, focusing on David's preparations for the Temple and his efforts to establish proper worship.
Second Chronicles highlights the reigns of the kings of Judah, emphasizing times of revival and reform, and concludes with the decree of Cyrus allowing return from exile.
Ezra records the return of Jewish exiles to Jerusalem under Zerubbabel and later Ezra himself, the rebuilding of the Temple, and spiritual reforms.
Nehemiah tells the story of rebuilding Jerusalem's walls in 52 days despite fierce opposition, followed by spiritual renewal through public reading of the Law.
Esther shows God's hidden providence as Queen Esther risked her life to save the Jewish people from Haman's plot, though God's name is never mentioned.
Job grapples with undeserved suffering through poetic dialogues, ultimately revealing that God's wisdom transcends human understanding and that He is sovereign in all things.
The Psalms are 150 songs and prayers covering the full range of human experience (praise, lament, thanksgiving, and trust), guiding God's people in heartfelt worship.
Proverbs is a collection of wise sayings for living well, emphasizing that true wisdom begins with fearing God and shapes every area of daily life.
Ecclesiastes explores the emptiness of life 'under the sun' apart from God, concluding that fearing God and keeping His commandments is the whole purpose of humanity.
The Song of Solomon celebrates the beauty of romantic love between a bride and groom, often read as a metaphor for the love between God and His people.
Isaiah contains some of the most majestic prophecies in Scripture: judgment on Judah and the nations, comfort for exiles, and vivid descriptions of the Suffering Servant who brings salvation.
Called the 'weeping prophet,' Jeremiah preached God's judgment to a stubborn Judah, while also announcing the glorious promise of a new covenant written on the heart.
Lamentations is a collection of five poetic laments over the destruction of Jerusalem, expressing profound grief while holding on to the truth of God's faithfulness.
Ezekiel, a priest-prophet among the exiles in Babylon, received spectacular visions of God's glory, pronounced judgment on Jerusalem, and proclaimed future restoration.
Daniel blends narrative and apocalyptic visions to show that God is sovereign over empires and history, encouraging faithfulness under pressure through remarkable stories and prophecies.
God commanded Hosea to marry an unfaithful wife as a living parable of Israel's spiritual adultery, and God's relentless love that pursues and restores.
Joel uses a devastating locust plague as a call to repentance, warning of the coming Day of the Lord and promising the outpouring of the Holy Spirit on all people.
Amos, a shepherd turned prophet, declared God's judgment on Israel's social injustice, empty religion, and oppression of the poor, calling for justice to 'roll on like a river.'
The shortest book of the Hebrew Scriptures pronounces judgment on Edom for rejoicing over Jerusalem's fall, affirming that pride comes before destruction.
Jonah's story of fleeing God's call, being swallowed by a great fish, and ultimately going to Nineveh reveals God's compassion extending beyond Israel to all nations.
Micah alternates between judgment and hope, condemning corruption and injustice while prophesying the Messiah's birth in Bethlehem and calling for justice, mercy, and humility.
Nahum declares the fall of Nineveh (the mighty Assyrian capital) as a demonstration that God is slow to anger but will not leave the guilty unpunished.
Habakkuk wrestles honestly with God about evil and injustice, and receives the answer that 'the righteous shall live by faith,' even when circumstances make no sense.
Zephaniah warns of the coming Day of the Lord's judgment on Judah and the nations, but concludes with a joyful promise that God will rejoice over His restored people with singing.
Haggai delivered four short messages urging the returned exiles to stop neglecting the Temple and rebuild it, promising that God's glory in the new Temple would surpass the old.
Zechariah encouraged the rebuilding of the Temple through eight night visions and rich Messianic prophecies about a humble King entering Jerusalem on a donkey.
Malachi, the last voice of the Hebrew Scriptures, rebukes priests and people for spiritual apathy and covenant unfaithfulness, closing with a promise of the coming messenger.
Matthew presents Jesus as the fulfillment of Hebrew Scripture prophecy and the long-awaited King of Israel, structured around five major discourses including the Sermon on the Mount.
The shortest gospel moves at a rapid pace, emphasizing Jesus' powerful actions and miracles. Mark presents Jesus as the Suffering Servant who came to serve and give His life.
Luke's careful, orderly account emphasizes Jesus' compassion for the poor, outcasts, women, and Gentiles, showing that salvation in Christ is available to all people.
John's deeply theological gospel uses seven signs and seven 'I AM' statements to reveal Jesus as the eternal Son of God, written so readers would believe and have life in His name.
Acts traces the spread of the gospel from Jerusalem to Rome through the power of the Holy Spirit, following the apostles Peter and Paul as the church expands to all nations.
Romans is Paul's most systematic presentation of the gospel: the condemnation of all humanity in sin, the righteousness of God through faith in Christ, and new life in the Spirit.
Paul addresses serious problems in the Corinthian church (divisions, immorality, lawsuits, spiritual gifts, and doubts about resurrection), calling them to unity and love.
Paul's most personal letter defends his apostleship and ministry amid intense suffering, teaching that God's power is made perfect in weakness.
Paul passionately defends the gospel of grace against those adding circumcision as a requirement for salvation, declaring that we are justified by faith alone, not law-keeping.
Ephesians soars with rich teaching on the believer's identity in Christ, the unity of the church as Christ's body, and the call to walk in love while standing firm in spiritual warfare.
Written from prison, Philippians radiates joy in every chapter. Paul urges the church to have the same humble mind as Christ and to find contentment in all circumstances.
Colossians counters early heresy by exalting Christ as the image of God and the head of creation and the church. Complete in Him, believers need nothing else.
Paul's earliest letter encourages young believers facing persecution, instructs them on holy living, and gives hope about the resurrection and Christ's return.
Paul corrects misunderstandings about the Day of the Lord (which had not yet come), encouraging endurance under persecution and faithful, diligent living.
Paul writes his young protégé Timothy with instructions for church order, leadership qualifications, and sound doctrine against false teaching in Ephesus.
Paul's final letter, written near death, urges Timothy to remain faithful, preach the Word boldly, and endure suffering, passing on the faith to the next generation.
Paul instructs Titus in organizing the churches of Crete by appointing qualified leaders and teaching sound doctrine that produces godly living.
This brief personal letter appeals to Philemon to receive back his runaway slave Onesimus (now a fellow believer) as a brother in Christ, not as a slave.
Hebrews presents Jesus as superior to angels, Moses, and the Levitical priesthood. It calls Jewish Christians not to drift from faith, supported by a magnificent 'hall of faith.'
James is a practical wisdom letter insisting that genuine saving faith will naturally produce good works, taming the tongue, caring for the poor, and persevering through trials.
Peter writes to scattered, persecuted believers, reminding them of their living hope through Christ's resurrection and calling them to holy conduct among the Gentiles.
Peter warns against false teachers who distort the truth, urges spiritual growth in knowledge of Christ, and assures believers that the Lord will return as promised.
John writes to combat early Gnosticism by affirming the physical reality of Jesus and testing true faith by obedience, love for one another, and confession that Jesus is Lord.

The shortest book in the Christian Greek Scriptures warns a local church not to offer hospitality to false teachers who deny that Jesus Christ came in the flesh, while urging continued love and obedience.
John commends Gaius for welcoming traveling missionaries, rebukes the domineering Diotrephes who refuses them, and upholds Demetrius as a model of faithfulness.
Jude urgently calls believers to defend the faith against false teachers who have secretly infiltrated the church, promising judgment on the ungodly and mercy for the faithful.
Revelation is a prophetic vision of Christ's ultimate victory over evil, the judgment of the nations, and the creation of a new heaven and new earth where God dwells with His people forever.

about.heroSub: Track your reading, connect with fellow students, and grow in your love for God's Word.
about.purposeBody1: JW Study was built with one simple goal: give Bible students a persistent, cross-device tracker for their Bible reading. Whether you're on your phone at the Kingdom Hall, on your laptop at home, or on a tablet during family worship, your progress is always with you.
about.purposeBody2: Beyond tracking, we wanted to create a space where people studying with Jehovah's Witnesses (and active Witnesses themselves) can encourage one another, share thoughts, and grow together as a community of Bible students.
about.card1Body: Those who are studying with Jehovah's Witnesses and want to follow along in the New World Translation as they learn.
about.card2Body: Jehovah's Witnesses looking to log their personal Bible reading and connect with a community that shares the same values.
about.card3Body: Families doing family worship, or study groups wanting to encourage each other's reading progress and share insights.
about.jwBody: JW Study is designed as a companion to the JW Library app. Do your reading in JW Library (where you can access a free digital copy of the New World Translation), then come here to log your chapters, take notes, and share your journey with the community.
about.feat1: Track every chapter: mark chapters read across all 66 books of the Bible, with your progress saved to the cloud so it follows you everywhere.
about.feat2: Save personal notes: write private notes tied to any book and chapter as you study.
about.feat3: Join the community forum: discuss Bible topics, ask questions, and encourage fellow students.
about.feat4: Test your knowledge: take Bible quizzes and earn badges as you grow in your understanding of Scripture.
about.feat5: Follow other readers: see activity from the people you follow and share your own updates with your community.
about.feat6: Share your progress: generate a shareable image of your reading progress to encourage others.
about.creatorBio: Lexx is a Bible student and Jehovah's Witness who built JW Study out of a personal need: a simple, reliable way to track Bible reading across any device, without losing progress. What started as a personal tool grew into a full community platform for others walking the same spiritual journey.
about.ctaSub: Create a free account and begin tracking your Bible reading today.
about.footer: JW Study is an independent community project and is not affiliated with or endorsed by Jehovah's Witnesses or the Watch Tower Society.

verses.text: For God loved the world so much that he gave his only-begotten Son, so that everyone exercising faith in him might not be destroyed but have everlasting life.
For I well know the thoughts that I am thinking toward you,' declares Jehovah, 'thoughts of peace, and not of calamity, to give you a future and a hope.
For all things I have the strength through the one who gives me power.
We know that God makes all his works cooperate together for the good of those who love God, those who are the ones called according to his purpose.
Trust in Jehovah with all your heart, and do not rely on your own understanding. In all your ways take notice of him, and he will make your paths straight.
But those hoping in Jehovah will regain power. They will soar up with wings like eagles. They will run and not grow weary; they will walk and not tire out.
Jehovah is my Shepherd. I will lack nothing.
Keep on, then, seeking first the Kingdom and his righteousness, and all these other things will be added to you.

By this undeserved kindness you have been saved through faith, and this is not of your own doing; rather, it is God's gift. No, it is not a result of works, so that no one should have grounds for boasting.

All Scripture is inspired of God and beneficial for teaching, for reproving, for setting things straight, for disciplining in righteousness, so that the man of God may be fully competent, completely equipped for every good work.
But he was pierced for our transgression; he was crushed for our errors. He bore the punishment for our peace, and because of his wounds we were healed.
It is because of Jehovah's loyal love that we have not come to our finish, for his mercies never end. They are new each morning; great is your faithfulness!
Have I not commanded you? Be courageous and strong. Do not be struck with terror or filled with dread, for Jehovah your God is with you wherever you go.
God is our refuge and strength, a help that is readily found in times of distress.

For I am convinced that neither death nor life nor angels nor governments nor things now here nor things to come nor powers nor height nor depth nor any other creation will be able to separate us from God's love that is in Christ Jesus our Lord.
Come to me, all you who are toiling and loaded down, and I will refresh you.
Now, however, these three remain: faith, hope, love, the greatest of these being love.
Faith is the assured expectation of what is hoped for, the evident demonstration of realities that are not seen.
He has told you, O man, what is good. And what is Jehovah requiring of you? Only to exercise justice, to cherish loyalty, and to walk in modesty with your God!
Your word is a lamp to my foot, and a light for my path.
Jesus said to him: 'I am the way and the truth and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me.'
Therefore, if anyone is in union with Christ, he is a new creation; the old things passed away; look! new things have come into existence.
And stop being molded by this system of things, but be transformed by making your mind over, so that you may prove to yourselves the good and acceptable and perfect will of God.
Listen, O Israel: Jehovah our God is one Jehovah. You must love Jehovah your God with all your heart and all your soul and all your strength.
The thief comes only to steal and slaughter and destroy. I have come that they may have life and have it in abundance.
I praise you because in an awe-inspiring way I am wonderfully made. Your works are wonderful; I know this very well.

Do not be anxious over anything, but in everything by prayer and supplication along with thanksgiving, let your petitions be made known to God; and the peace of God that surpasses all understanding will guard your hearts and your mental powers by means of Christ Jesus.
In the beginning God created the heavens and the earth.
But the righteous one will live by his faithfulness.
I will give you a new heart and put a new spirit within you, and I will remove the heart of stone from your body and give you a heart of flesh.
Jehovah your God is in your midst, a mighty one who will save. He will rejoice over you with great joy. He will become silent in his love. He will exult over you with a joyful shout.
Whoever does not love has not come to know God, because God is love.
However, become doers of the word and not hearers only, deceiving yourselves with false reasoning.
Then if my people who are called by my name humble themselves and pray and seek my face and turn away from their evil ways, I will hear from the heavens and forgive their sin and heal their land.
She is clothed with strength and splendor, and she looks to the future with a smile.
Whatever you are doing, whether you speak or act, do everything in the name of the Lord Jesus, thanking God the Father through him.
But you will receive power when the holy spirit comes upon you, and you will be witnesses of me in Jerusalem, in all Judea and Samaria, and to the most distant part of the earth.
Where you go I will go, and where you spend the night I will spend the night. Your people will be my people and your God my God.
And who knows whether it is for a time like this that you have attained to your royal status?
For I know that my Redeemer is alive, and that in the end he will stand up over the dust.

If it must be, our God whom we serve is able to rescue us from the burning fiery furnace, O king, and he will rescue us from your hand. But even if he does not, let it be known to you, O king, that we will not serve your gods.
while you throw all your anxiety on him, because he cares for you.
For the joy of Jehovah is your stronghold.
Find exquisite delight in Jehovah, and he will grant you the desires of your heart.
Do not be afraid, for I am with you. Do not be anxious, for I am your God. I will fortify you, yes, I will help you, I will really hold on to you with my right hand of righteousness.
You are the light of the world. A city cannot be hid when located on a mountain.
I am the vine; you are the branches. Whoever remains in union with me and I in union with him, this one bears much fruit; for apart from me you can do nothing at all.
And he will wipe out every tear from their eyes, and death will be no more, neither will mourning nor outcry nor pain be anymore. The former things have passed away.
But God recommends his own love to us in that, while we were yet sinners, Christ died for us.
Taste and see that Jehovah is good; happy is the man who takes refuge in him.
But let justice flow down like waters, and righteousness like an ever-flowing stream.
Jehovah is not slow concerning his promise, as some people consider slowness, but he is patient with you because he does not desire anyone to be destroyed but desires all to attain to repentance.
For even the Son of man came, not to be ministered to, but to minister and to give his life as a ransom in exchange for many.
For the Son of man came to seek and to save what was lost.

For the word of God is alive and exerts power and is sharper than any two-edged sword and pierces even to the dividing of soul and spirit, and of joints from the marrow, and is able to discern thoughts and intentions of the heart.
Always be rejoicing. Pray constantly. Give thanks for everything. This is the will of God in union with Christ Jesus for you.
After that I will pour out my spirit on every sort of flesh, and your sons and your daughters will certainly prophesy, your old men will dream dreams, and your young men will see visions.
'Not by a military force, nor by power, but by my spirit,' says Jehovah of armies.

Go, therefore, and make disciples of people of all the nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the holy spirit, teaching them to observe all the things I have commanded you.
As far off as the sunrise is from the sunset, so far off from us he has put our transgressions.
For a child has been born to us, a son has been given to us; and the rulership will rest on his shoulder. His name will be called Wonderful Counselor, Mighty God, Eternal Father, Prince of Peace.
I found it necessary to write you to urge you to put up a hard fight for the faith that was once for all time delivered to the holy ones.

Bring the entire tithe into the storehouse, so that there may be food in my house; and test me out, please, in this regard,' says Jehovah of armies, 'to see whether I will not open to you the floodgates of the heavens and pour out on you a blessing until there is nothing lacking.

Happy is the man who does not walk according to the advice of the wicked and does not stand on the path of sinners and does not sit in the seat of scoffers. But his delight is in the law of Jehovah, and he reads His law in an undertone day and night.

But he said to me: 'My undeserved kindness is sufficient for you, for my power is made perfect in weakness.' Most gladly, then, I will boast about my weaknesses, in order that the power of the Christ may remain over me like a tent.
Jehovah will fight for you, and you will keep silent.

course.closingBlurb: Personal study is one of the greatest gifts a Bible student has. Don't let this course end here.
course.intro: You will not just read about studying. You will do it, with real verses, real publications, real exercises, the way the Bereans did.
course.kicker: A nine-lesson course for Bible students
course.title: Learn to Study

body.bq1: Now the latter were more noble-minded than those in Thessalonica, for they accepted the word with the greatest eagerness of mind, carefully examining the Scriptures daily to see whether these things were so.
body.h3_1: Three marks of study that shapes a life

list1.item1: Regular. Rhythm beats intensity. Fifteen minutes daily outlasts a three-hour binge on Saturday.
list1.item2: Active. Pencil in hand. Question in mind. If you can't recall what you read an hour later, you weren't really studying.
list1.item3: Prayerful. Jehovah gives wisdom generously to those who ask (Jas. 1:5). Holy spirit isn't automatic, ask.

body.p1: When Paul preached in Beroea, the people there did something the apostle singled out for praise. They received the word eagerly, but they didn't stop there. They checked.
body.p2: That's the pattern for personal study. Eager reception and careful examination, every day. It's not enough to agree with what you hear at the meetings. The Bereans, and Paul himself, who wrote more of the Christian Greek Scriptures than anyone, wanted us digging in ourselves.
body.p3: This course is built around those three marks. You'll do short exercises with real scriptures, not just read about them. That's the Berean pattern in miniature.

ex.prompt1: What have you read in your Bible or the publications this past week?
ex.prompt2: Where did you catch a glimpse of Jehovah's qualities in the passage?
ex.prompt3: What is this passage asking you to change, start, or stop?
ex.prompt4: Write a one-sentence prayer turning the answer above into a conversation with Jehovah.
ex.title: Meditate: what moved you this week?

lesson1.oneLine: The Bereans didn't just listen. They examined. So should we.
lesson1.title: Why Personal Study Matters

body.bq1: Rule of thumb: JW Library for reading and meetings. WOL for researching and answering questions.
body.h3_1: JW Library: the faithful field companion
body.h3_2: Watchtower ONLINE LIBRARY (WOL): the research desk
body.p1: We're spoiled. Bible students before us pieced their study time together from paperback Bibles, mimeographed outlines, and trips to the Kingdom Hall library. Today, two apps do most of that work, and knowing which to reach for saves real time.
body.p2: JW Library is the New World Translation, the meeting media, the songbook, and the daily text in your pocket. It's portable, it works offline, and it's tuned for reading and meetings, not deep research.
body.p3: WOL is the full shelf. Every publication, indexed and cross-linked. It's where you go when a question opens up and you need to follow the thread, past Watchtower articles, Insight entries, Reasoning chapters, Yearbooks.

ex.intro: Each scenario shows up in real life. Which resource fits best?

q1.explanation: JW Library is built for offline, one-hand reading. The daily text lives on the home screen and the Bible is a tap away.
q1.scenario: You're on the bus and want to read the daily text and quickly review today's Bible chapter.

q2.explanation: WOL is the research library. Use its search with quotation marks around "Hezekiah" to get a ranked list across all publications.
q2.scenario: You want to find every Watchtower article that discusses King Hezekiah's reforms over the last twenty years.

q3.explanation: The NWT Study Edition in JW Library exposes study notes and cross-references right beside the verse. WOL has them too, but JW Library's Bible view is faster for a single passage.
q3.scenario: You're prepping a talk and want the Reference Bible's cross-references next to the verse you're working on.

q4.explanation: JW Library keeps meeting media available offline once you've downloaded it. WOL needs a connection.
q4.scenario: You want to review last week's meeting video when you're offline at a family member's home with no Wi-Fi.

q5.explanation: Insight is available in both, but WOL's cross-link experience is faster for the long encyclopedia-style entries where you'll jump between sub-entries.
q5.scenario: You want to read the complete Insight on the Scriptures entry on "Faith" with all its sub-sections and cross-links.

ex.title: Cross-reference: pick the right tool

lesson2.oneLine: Two brilliant tools. Different jobs. Knowing which to reach for is half the battle.
lesson2.title: JW Library vs. Watchtower ONLINE LIBRARY

body.p1: New Bible students sometimes assume all the publications say roughly the same thing. They don't. Each one is written for a different job. Here are the five you'll reach for most.
body.p2: The encyclopedia. Two volumes covering people, places, objects, concepts, alphabetically, with cross-references. When a name, a word, or a custom in the Scriptures stops you, Insight is the first stop.
body.p3: The field service companion. Organized by topic, "Blood," "Cross," "God," "Suffering", with direct answers and sample conversation lines. Built for objections and discussions.
body.p4: Current spiritual food. The Study Edition is meeting material; the Public Edition is written for the broader audience. When a question is about our current understanding, the most recent Watchtower is almost always where to check.
body.p5: Biblical principles applied to everyday life, family, work, health, anxiety, teens. The voice is warm and practical. Excellent for householders not yet ready for deep doctrinal discussion.
body.p6: One of a family of prophetic books (with Jeremiah, Isaiah's Prophecy, and Revelation's Grand Climax) that walks verse-by-verse through Ezekiel. Reach for these when studying a prophetic book.

ex.intro: A friend or householder asks you one of these. Which publication is the best starting point?

q1.explanation: Reasoning has a full chapter on "Suffering" with scriptures, logical reasoning, and sample conversational responses, built exactly for this question.
q1.scenario: "Why does a loving God allow suffering in the world?"

q2.explanation: Insight is the encyclopedia, its "Jehovah" entry covers etymology, pronunciation history, and every scriptural occurrence with depth.
q2.scenario: "What did the divine name Jehovah mean in ancient Hebrew, and how was it pronounced?"

q3.explanation: Awake! specializes in practical life topics like anxiety, with biblical principles presented warmly. A recent Watchtower study article would also be excellent.
q3.scenario: "I keep lying awake at night with anxiety. What does the Bible say I should do?"

q4.explanation: Start with Insight's entry on "Great Crowd" for the full scriptural exposition. The prophetic commentaries on Revelation go deeper, and Reasoning gives a shorter conversational version.
q4.scenario: "What is the 'great crowd' spoken of in Revelation 7, and how do we know it's a different group from the 144,000?"

q5.explanation: On questions where our understanding has been refined over time, the most recent Watchtower article carries the current spiritual food. Always check for the latest.
q5.scenario: "I want to understand the Bible's current direction on blood fractions for medical use."

ex.title: Cross-reference: which publication fits this question?

lesson3.oneLine: The faithful and discreet slave has given us a full shelf. Each book does one job exceptionally well.
lesson3.title: Insight, Reasoning & the Publications

unit1.oneLine: Before you learn to study deeply, you need to know what's in your hand and why it's there.
unit1.title: Your Study Toolkit

body.p1: S.O.A.P. is an acronym, Scripture, Observation, Application, Prayer, and it's one of the simplest, most durable frameworks for turning Bible reading into Bible study. It works for a single verse or a whole chapter.
body.p2: Write the verse out. By hand if you can. Copying forces you to slow down and notice things a speed-reader misses: the conjunctions, the imperatives, the conditions.
body.p3: What does the passage actually say? Who is speaking? To whom? When? What words repeat? This is not yet about you, it's about what's there on the page.
body.p4: Now turn toward yourself. What does this verse ask of me today? Where am I living against it? What would obedience look like this week?
body.p5: Pray the verse back to Jehovah. Thank him for what it reveals about him. Ask for the specific help the application requires.

ex.promptA: Where in your life are you currently 'relying on your own understanding'? What would trusting Jehovah look like there this week?
ex.promptO: What does this passage command? What does it promise? What's the condition?
ex.promptP: Turn your answer into a short prayer. Start with 'Jehovah,' and keep it honest.
ex.promptS: Copy Proverbs 3:5-6 out in your own handwriting, or type it slowly below.
ex.title: S.O.A.P. on Proverbs 3:5-6
ex.verseText: Trust in Jehovah with all your heart, and do not rely on your own understanding. In all your ways take notice of him, and he will make your paths straight.

lesson4.oneLine: Four letters. One of the most reliable scaffolds for a deep personal study session.
lesson4.title: The S.O.A.P. Method

list1.item1: Who, who's speaking, and who are they speaking to?
list1.item2: What, what is actually happening in the verse? What word gets repeated or emphasized?
list1.item3: When / Where, what's the historical or geographical context?
list1.item4: Why, why would Jehovah want this recorded? Why does it matter?
list1.item5: How, how does it apply to me, today, in my actual circumstances?

body.p1: A verse can look flat on a quick read. The same verse, put under the right questions, splits open. Here are five questions that almost always uncover something new.
body.p2: Run any verse through all five questions and you've done more thinking than most people do in a whole chapter of reading.

how.hint: Name one specific anxiety you'll 'throw' on Jehovah in prayer today.
how.prompt: How does this apply to you this week?

ex.title: Apply the five questions to 1 Peter 5:7
ex.verseText: Throw all your anxiety on him, because he cares for you.

what.hint: 'Throw', it's decisive, not passive. 'All' is the emphatic word.
what.prompt: What is the command, and what word gets emphasized?

whenWhere.hint: Written around 62–64 CE, to brothers and sisters facing growing Roman hostility.

who.hint: Peter, a fellow elder, writes to Christians scattered through Asia Minor.
who.prompt: Who is speaking, and to whom is he writing?

why.hint: Because anxiety isolates, and this verse is the corrective, we have a Father who cares.
why.prompt: Why would Jehovah want this recorded?

lesson5.oneLine: Good questions are the keys that open closed verses.
lesson5.title: Asking Questions That Open Verses

list1.item4: Gold, a quality of Jehovah or Jesus.

body.p1: Highlighting without a system is just decoration. Here's a simple five-color setup that's survived decades of Bible students, and works just as well in JW Library as it does in a paper Bible.
body.p2: The next time you sit with a Watchtower paragraph, slow down and mark it up. A single marked-up paragraph you remember is worth more than ten skimmed chapters you don't.

guide.command: "commands us to pray without ceasing", a command
guide.warning: "drifting leads to shipwreck of faith", a warning

lesson6.oneLine: Marks on the page are memory aids. A good system turns a paragraph into a map.
lesson6.title: Highlighting & Note-Taking That Works

unit2.oneLine: Now you've got the tools. Here's how to actually use them.
unit2.title: The Art of Studying

list1.item1: Marginal references in the NWT Reference Bible, right-column, for every significant verse.
list1.item2: WOL's cited-by feature, shows every publication that cites the verse you're on.
list1.item3: Insight on the Scriptures, topic entries link to all major passages on the topic.

body.p1: Jehovah wrote his Word in a way that talks to itself. A verse in Genesis connects to one in Revelation. A phrase in Psalms lights up a story in Kings. Cross-referencing is the skill of following those connections, and the NWT Reference Edition is designed for it.
body.p2: Build a chain. Start with a verse. Follow one reference. Then follow one from there. After three or four hops, you'll see a pattern you never would have seen in the starting verse alone.

how.hint: Be concrete, a specific time, a specific chapter, a specific prayer.
how.prompt: What one thing will you do this week to 'keep on coming to know' Jehovah?

ex.title: Build a chain from John 17:3
ex.verseText: This means everlasting life, their coming to know you, the only true God, and the one whom you sent, Jesus Christ.

what.hint: 2 Peter 3:18, 'keep on growing in the undeserved kindness and knowledge of our Lord.'
what.prompt: What does 'coming to know' imply? Find a verse that describes knowing God as ongoing, not a one-time event.

whenWhere.hint: 1 Corinthians 8:6, 'there is actually to us one God, the Father... and there is one Lord, Jesus Christ.'
whenWhere.prompt: The verse distinguishes 'the only true God' from Jesus. Find another verse that makes the same distinction.

who.hint: Matthew 26:39 is one of the most intense, compare his posture toward the Father.
who.prompt: Who is speaking here? Find a cross-reference to another prayer of Jesus and note the verse.

why.hint: James 4:8, 'draw close to God, and he will draw close to you.'
why.prompt: Why does Jesus frame 'everlasting life' as a relationship rather than a reward? Find a verse that shows Jehovah's heart toward us.

lesson7.oneLine: Scripture is its own best commentary. Learning to chain passages is learning to think like a Berean.
lesson7.title: Cross-Referencing Like a Berean

body.bq1: His delight is in the law of Jehovah, and he reads His law in an undertone day and night.

list1.item1: Pick one verse. Just one. Don't rush to the next.
list1.item2: Read it three times, slowly. Pause after each read.
list1.item3: Sit with it. Let a question rise. Let a feeling rise. Let Jehovah speak.

body.p1: The Hebrew word translated 'reads in an undertone' carries the image of a cow chewing cud, slow, repeated, patient. Meditation is that slow chewing. You won't get the nutrients from a verse you swallow whole.
body.p2: Reading asks, "What does it say?" Meditation asks, "What does it mean, and what does it do to me?" One fills the mind. The other shapes the person.

introVerse.text: Jehovah is my Shepherd. I will lack nothing.

ex.prompt1: Read the verse three times slowly. Let the second reading be slower than the first.
ex.prompt2: What image does the word 'Shepherd' raise for you? What does it tell you about how Jehovah relates to you?
ex.prompt3: Where in your life are you currently afraid you will lack something? Hold that with the verse.
ex.prompt4: Write one sentence to Jehovah about what you just saw.
ex.title: Guided 3-minute meditation

lesson8.oneLine: Reading is the meal. Meditation is the digestion. Most of the nutrition happens here.
lesson8.title: Meditation, Letting the Word Sink In

list1.item1: Daily (15 min): Bible reading + daily text. Same time, same place.
list1.item2: Mid-week (45 min): Meeting prep, read the Watchtower study article, highlight, mark up.
list1.item3: Weekend (60 min): One S.O.A.P. study on a chapter of your choice.
list1.item4: Monthly: Pick one Insight entry to read all the way through.

body.p1: We've covered the tools and the techniques. Now for the hardest part: keeping it going. Habits, not heroics, build a Bible student.
body.p2: This isn't a prescription. It's a frame. Shrink it or expand it to fit your life, but write down what you commit to, and then keep showing up.
body.p3: All glory to Jehovah. He has given us his Word, his spirit, and his organization. The rest is just showing up.

ex.prompt1: What time of day will you study? (Be specific, e.g. '7:00 AM, before breakfast.')
ex.prompt2: Which days of the week? (Start smaller than you think, three days you actually do beats seven you don't.)
ex.prompt3: Where will you study? (Same spot every time, if possible.)
ex.prompt4: Which Bible book will you start with? (If unsure, Mark is short. James is practical. Psalms is rich.)
ex.prompt5: Write one sentence of commitment to Jehovah. You're not promising perfection, you're promising to show up.
ex.title: Your commitment

lesson9.oneLine: The best study method is the one you'll still be doing a year from now.
lesson9.title: Building Your Study Routine

meta.title: Privacy Policy | JW Study
meta.description: Read the JW Study privacy policy, how we collect, use, and protect your personal information.

section1.heading: 1. Overview
section1.body1: JW Study ("we", "us", "our") is committed to protecting your privacy. This Privacy Policy explains what information we collect, how we use it, and your rights regarding that information when you use our App at jwstudy.org.
section1.body2: JW Study is a free service. We do not sell your data, monetize your information, or use it for advertising purposes of any kind.

section2.heading: 2. Information We Collect
section2.item1Body: When you register, we collect your email address and any optional display name or profile photo you provide.
section2.item2Body: We store which Bible chapters you have marked as read so your progress syncs across devices.
section2.item3Body: Blog posts, forum threads, replies, comments, notes, and profile information you create are stored and associated with your account.
section2.item4Body: Messages you send to the AI Study Companion are transmitted to our AI provider (Anthropic) to generate a response and are not stored permanently on our servers. We log aggregate usage counts (number of AI requests per user) for abuse prevention and service improvement. The content of AI conversations is not stored or shared publicly.
section2.item5Body: Submitted content may be scanned for profanity and for links to apostate or ex-JW sources that conflict with Jehovah's Witness teachings (such links are blocked and not stored). You are responsible for any personal information (email, phone number, social links, etc.) you choose to share in your posts, messages, or profile, do not share information you would not want other members of the community to see.
section2.item6Body: If you enable push notifications, we store a device notification token to deliver alerts to your device. You can revoke this at any time through your browser or device settings.
section2.item7Body: We use Google Analytics (GA4) and Vercel Analytics to collect anonymized data about how the App is used (pages visited, session duration, device type, general location by country). No personally identifiable information is sent to these analytics services.
section2.item8Body: We use Sentry to capture application errors and performance issues. Error reports may include browser type, OS, and a stack trace of the error, but do not include the content of your posts or messages.
section2.item9Body: Standard server logs including IP address and browser type may be collected by our hosting provider (Vercel) and database provider (Supabase).

section3.heading: 3. How We Use Your Information
section3.item1: To provide and maintain the App and your account
section3.item2: To sync your reading progress across devices
section3.item3: To send transactional emails (email confirmation, password reset)
section3.item4: To deliver push notifications you have opted into
section3.item5: To display your public profile and content to other users
section3.item6: To power the AI Study Companion by passing your messages to our AI provider
section3.item7: To improve the App using anonymized analytics and error monitoring
section3.item8: To prevent abuse and enforce our community standards
section3.item9: To investigate violations and cooperate with law enforcement where required by law
section3.footer3: use your data for advertising. We do
section3.footer4: share your data with any third party except as described in Section 4.

section4.heading: 4. Third-Party Services
section4.intro: We use the following third-party services to operate the App. Each has its own privacy policy governing their data handling:
section4.item3Body: , AI language model powering the AI Study Companion (anthropic.com/privacy)
section4.item5Body: , anonymized usage analytics; no PII is shared (policies.google.com/privacy)

section5.heading: 5. AI Data Handling
section5.body1: The AI Study Companion sends your messages to Anthropic's API to generate responses. Anthropic's data handling practices are governed by their privacy policy. We do not permanently store the content of your AI conversations on our servers. We store only a usage count per user for abuse prevention purposes.
section5.body2: AI-generated blog drafts that you choose to publish become user content subject to the same terms as any other content you create.
section5.body3Strong: Do not share sensitive personal information in AI conversations.
section5.body3Rest: Treat the AI Study Companion as you would any cloud-based service.

section6.heading: 6. Data Retention
section6.body: Your data is retained for as long as your account is active. If you delete your account, your personal information and content will be removed from our systems within a reasonable timeframe, except where retention is required by law, including data we are legally required to preserve in connection with reports made to law enforcement or child safety authorities.

section7.heading: 7. Your Rights
section7.intro: Depending on your location, you may have the right to:
section7.item1: Access the personal data we hold about you
section7.item2: Correct inaccurate data via your profile settings
section7.item3: Request deletion of your account and data
section7.item4: Request a copy of your data
section7.item5: Opt out of push notifications at any time through your browser or device settings
section7.footer: To exercise any of these rights, contact us through the App's forum or via jwstudy.org.

section8.heading: 8. Cookies & Local Storage
section8.body: The App uses browser localStorage to store your theme preference, reading progress cache, and AI chat history for a better experience. Vercel Analytics collects anonymized, cookie-free usage data. Google Analytics uses cookies to measure usage, you can opt out via browser settings or the Google Analytics opt-out browser add-on.

section9.heading: 🛡️ 9. Children's Privacy & Child Safety
section9.ageBody: This App is intended for users aged 13 and older. Users between 13 and 17 must have a parent or guardian's permission. We do not knowingly collect personal information from children under 13. If you believe a child under 13 has created an account, please contact us immediately so we can remove it.
section9.commitmentBody: We take the safety of children on our platform with the utmost seriousness. We actively monitor for and prohibit any content or behavior that could harm, exploit, or sexualize minors. This includes Child Sexual Abuse Material (CSAM), grooming, or any attempt to solicit personal information from minors.
section9.reportingBody: We are legally required, and morally committed, to report any apparent child sexual exploitation material or grooming activity discovered on our platform to the appropriate authorities, including:
section9.item1Body: (CyberTipLine.org), as required by U.S. federal law (18 U.S.C. § 2258A)
section9.item2Body: in the jurisdiction where the violation occurred
section9.dataNote: When making such reports, we provide all relevant data including account information, IP addresses, timestamps, and content. This data sharing is required by law and is not subject to the privacy protections described elsewhere in this policy.
section9.parentalBody: We encourage parents and guardians to review their child's use of this App. If you have concerns about content or interactions your child has encountered on JW Study, please report it using the in-app report function or contact us directly.

section10.heading: 10. Law Enforcement Requests
section10.body: We may disclose your information to law enforcement, government agencies, or other authorized parties when required by law, court order, or when we believe in good faith that disclosure is necessary to prevent harm, protect the safety of any person, or respond to an emergency.

section11.heading: 11. Changes to This Policy
section11.body: We may update this Privacy Policy from time to time. We will update the "Last updated" date at the top of this page. Continued use of the App after changes are posted constitutes your acceptance of the updated policy.

section12.heading: 12. Contact
section12.body: If you have any questions or concerns about this Privacy Policy, please reach out via the community forum or through jwstudy.org.

meta.title: Terms of Service | JW Study
meta.description: Review the JW Study terms of service governing use of the Bible reading tracker and community features.

section1.heading: 1. Acceptance of Terms
section1.body: By accessing or using JW Study ("the App") at jwstudy.org, you agree to be bound by these Terms of Service. If you do not agree, please do not use the App.

section2.heading: 2. Description of Service
section2.body1: JW Study is a free Bible reading tracker and community platform designed for readers of the New World Translation. The App provides the following features at no charge to registered users:

tracker.text: , track your personal progress through all 66 books of the NWT

blog.text: , read and write JW-focused articles, leave comments

forum.text: , participate in discussions organized by category

ai.text: , an AI assistant drawing exclusively from JW publications and wol.jw.org

meeting.text: , tools to prepare for midweek and weekend congregation meetings

videos.text: , curated JW video content from approved creators

quizzes.text: , Bible knowledge quizzes based on NWT content

profiles.text: , public profiles showing your reading milestones and contributions

body2.bold: JW Study is completely free of charge.
body2.text: There are no paid tiers, no premium subscriptions, and no features locked behind a paywall. All registered users have full access to all features.

section2.body3: We reserve the right to modify, suspend, or discontinue any part of the service at any time.

section3.heading: 3. User Accounts & Age Requirements
section3.body1: You must create an account to access most features. You are responsible for maintaining the confidentiality of your account credentials and for all activity that occurs under your account. You must provide accurate information when registering.

body2.text: You must be at least 13 years of age to use this App. Users between the ages of 13 and 17 must have the permission of a parent or legal guardian. By creating an account, you confirm that you meet this requirement and, if under 18, that a parent or guardian has reviewed and agreed to these Terms on your behalf.

section3.body3: If we discover that an account belongs to a user under 13, we will terminate that account and remove associated data without notice.

section4.heading: 4. User Content
section4.body1: You retain ownership of content you create (blog posts, forum threads, replies, comments, notes, and profile information). By posting content, you grant JW Study a non-exclusive, royalty-free license to display that content within the App.
section4.body2: You agree not to post content that is:

list.item1: Hateful, abusive, harassing, or threatening toward any person or group
list.item2: Sexually explicit or pornographic in any form
list.item3: Harmful, exploitative, or sexualizing toward minors in any way
list.item5: Infringing on another person's intellectual property
list.item7: Profane or using language that is obscene, vulgar, or offensive
list.item8: Doctrine or teachings contrary to or critical of the Watch Tower Bible and Tract Society or Jehovah's Witnesses

section5.heading: 5. AI Study Companion
section5.body1: The AI Study Companion is an AI-powered assistant available to all registered users. It draws exclusively from Watch Tower publications and jw.org/wol.jw.org content to answer Bible questions and assist with JW study.

list.item1: The AI is designed to provide study assistance aligned with JW teachings only
list.item2: Conversations with the AI are used only to generate responses, they are not shared publicly
list.item3: Do not use the AI to generate content that violates these Terms, including harmful, illegal, or apostate material
list.item4: Do not attempt to manipulate the AI into producing content outside its intended purpose

section5.body2: AI-generated content, including blog drafts, is provided as a starting point and should be reviewed before publishing. JW Study does not guarantee the accuracy of AI responses and encourages users to verify all content against official JW publications.

section6.heading: 6. Personal Information & Link Policy
section6.body1: To protect the privacy and safety of all users, especially minors, the following are strictly prohibited in all user-generated content, including forum posts, blog posts, comments, profile bios, and display names:

email.text: , do not share your own or anyone else's email

phone.text: , including mobile, landline, or WhatsApp numbers

address.text: , street addresses, home addresses, or meeting locations

social.text: , links to or @username handles for Facebook, Instagram, Twitter/X, TikTok, Snapchat, YouTube, LinkedIn, Telegram, WhatsApp, Discord, or similar platforms

insecure.text: , only secure links beginning with https:// are permitted

section6.body2: These restrictions exist to keep this a safe, private environment and to prevent the App from being used to exchange contact information or redirect users to external platforms. Our systems automatically scan all content before submission and will block any post containing prohibited information.

section7.heading: 🛡️ 7. Child Safety Policy
section7.body1: The safety of children is a non-negotiable priority at JW Study. We have a zero-tolerance policy for any content or behavior that endangers, exploits, or sexualizes minors.
section7.body2: The following are strictly prohibited and will result in immediate permanent ban:

list1.item1: Child Sexual Abuse Material (CSAM) of any kind, images, text, video, or links
list1.item2: Grooming behavior, attempting to build inappropriate relationships with minors to gain their trust for exploitation
list1.item3: Soliciting personal information from minors, including age, location, school, or contact details
list1.item4: Any sexual content directed at or involving minors
list1.item5: Attempting to move a conversation with a minor to another platform for the purpose of exploitation

body3.bold: We will report violations to authorities.
body3.text: Any content or behavior that we identify as involving the sexual exploitation of children will be reported immediately to:

item1.bold: The National Center for Missing & Exploited Children (NCMEC)
item1.text: via the CyberTipline at CyberTipLine.org, as required by U.S. federal law (18 U.S.C. § 2258A)

item2.text: and relevant national authorities in the user's jurisdiction

item3.text: for international cases involving child sexual abuse imagery

section7.body4: We preserve all relevant account data, IP addresses, timestamps, and content associated with reported violations and will cooperate fully with law enforcement investigations.

body5.bold: If you witness behavior that may endanger a child on this platform, please report it immediately
body5.text: using the report function available on any post or profile, or contact us directly. If you believe a child is in immediate danger, contact your local emergency services (911 in the US) first.

section8.heading: 8. Community Standards
section8.body1: JW Study is a spiritually-focused community platform. We ask that all users engage respectfully and in a manner consistent with the values of the community. Debates intended to discourage faith or disrespect other users' beliefs are not permitted.
section8.body2: This is a community built on mutual respect and shared faith. Please keep all discussions uplifting, constructive, and free of hostility.

section9.heading: 9. Enforcement & Account Bans
section9.body1: Violations of these Terms, including posting prohibited content, attempting to circumvent automated filters, or engaging in harmful behavior toward other users, may result in:

removal.text: , violating posts, comments, or profile content will be removed without notice

suspension.text: , your account may be suspended pending review

ban.text: , serious or repeated violations will result in a permanent ban with no right to appeal

report.text: , violations involving child safety or illegal content will be reported to law enforcement and relevant agencies without prior notice

section9.body2: We reserve the right to take enforcement action at our sole discretion. We are not obligated to issue warnings before taking action.

section10.heading: 10. Intellectual Property
section10.body: The JW Study name, logo, design, and original code are the intellectual property of Lexx Solutionz. Scripture quotations are from the New World Translation of the Holy Scriptures and are used for personal and community study purposes. All Watch Tower publications referenced within the App remain the intellectual property of the Watch Tower Bible and Tract Society of Pennsylvania.

section11.heading: 11. No Affiliation with Watch Tower
section11.body: JW Study is an independent study tool created by and for Jehovah's Witnesses. It is not affiliated with, endorsed by, or operated by the Watch Tower Bible and Tract Society of Pennsylvania, the Governing Body of Jehovah's Witnesses, or any official JW organization. All doctrinal content is intended to be consistent with official JW teachings, but users should rely on official jw.org materials for authoritative guidance.

section12.heading: 12. Disclaimer of Warranties
section12.body: The App is provided "as is" without warranties of any kind. We do not guarantee that the App will be error-free, uninterrupted, or that data will never be lost. Use the App at your own risk.

section13.heading: 13. Limitation of Liability
section13.body: To the fullest extent permitted by law, JW Study and Lexx Solutionz shall not be liable for any indirect, incidental, or consequential damages arising from your use of the App.

section14.heading: 14. Changes to Terms
section14.body: We may update these Terms at any time. Continued use of the App after changes are posted constitutes acceptance of the updated Terms. We will update the "Last updated" date at the top of this page.

section15.heading: 15. Contact
section15.body: If you have questions about these Terms, you can reach us through the App's community forum or via the contact information listed on jwstudy.org.
